verses.text: 'Remember, O LORD, what is come upon us': their inheritance is given to strangers, and they are left orphans and widows. They appeal to God's memory. In utter loss, the first plea is that God would not forget.

'Remember, O LORD, what is come upon us': their inheritance is given to strangers, and they are left orphans and widows. They appeal to God's memory. In utter loss, the first plea is that God would not forget.

'Remember, O LORD, what is come upon us': their inheritance is given to strangers, and they are left orphans and widows. They appeal to God's memory. In utter loss, the first plea is that God would not forget.
They pay for their own water and wood, are driven hard, and stretch out their hands to Egypt and Assyria for bread. Dependence and shame. Sin has reduced the free to beggary.
They pay for their own water and wood, are driven hard, and stretch out their hands to Egypt and Assyria for bread. Dependence and shame. Sin has reduced the free to beggary.
They pay for their own water and wood, are driven hard, and stretch out their hands to Egypt and Assyria for bread. Dependence and shame. Sin has reduced the free to beggary.
'Our fathers have sinned... and we have borne their iniquities'; they risk their lives for bread amid the sword. A heavy inheritance. The consequences of sin can fall on more than one generation.
'Our fathers have sinned... and we have borne their iniquities'; they risk their lives for bread amid the sword. A heavy inheritance. The consequences of sin can fall on more than one generation.
'Our fathers have sinned... and we have borne their iniquities'; they risk their lives for bread amid the sword. A heavy inheritance. The consequences of sin can fall on more than one generation.
Famine blackens their skin; women are violated, princes hanged, elders dishonoured. No one is spared the shame. Judgment levels the high and the low together.
Famine blackens their skin; women are violated, princes hanged, elders dishonoured. No one is spared the shame. Judgment levels the high and the low together.
Famine blackens their skin; women are violated, princes hanged, elders dishonoured. No one is spared the shame. Judgment levels the high and the low together.
Young men grind at the mill, boys stagger under wood; the elders have left the gate, and 'the joy of our heart is ceased.' Their mirth turns to mourning. Sin drains away all gladness.
Young men grind at the mill, boys stagger under wood; the elders have left the gate, and 'the joy of our heart is ceased.' Their mirth turns to mourning. Sin drains away all gladness.
Young men grind at the mill, boys stagger under wood; the elders have left the gate, and 'the joy of our heart is ceased.' Their mirth turns to mourning. Sin drains away all gladness.
'The crown is fallen from our head: woe unto us, that we have sinned!' Their heart faints, and jackals roam desolate Zion. They name the cause. Honour lost is traced back to their own sin.
'The crown is fallen from our head: woe unto us, that we have sinned!' Their heart faints, and jackals roam desolate Zion. They name the cause. Honour lost is traced back to their own sin.
'The crown is fallen from our head: woe unto us, that we have sinned!' Their heart faints, and jackals roam desolate Zion. They name the cause. Honour lost is traced back to their own sin.

Yet they look up: 'Thou, O LORD, remainest for ever'; and pray, 'Turn thou us unto thee... renew our days as of old.' Hope rests on the eternal God. The book ends pleading for the grace that alone can restore.
